title: Between Independence and Freedom
gist: Historian Elisabeth Leake traces a persistent tension in US foreign policy between 'independence' (sovereign statehood) and 'freedom' (rights and equality), arguing the Declaration of Independence never promised the latter, and that from Manifest Destiny through Cold War self-determination doctrine to the 1971 Bangladesh genocide, Washington has repeatedly championed other states' independence while sidestepping their citizens' freedom — a tension she sees resurfacing in today's fractured 'America First.'

themes.theme: A founding tension, not a founding clarity

insights.claim: The Declaration of Independence never explicitly uses the words 'freedom' or 'American,' and its concluding paragraph refers to the colonies in the plural as 'Free and Independent States' — evidence the document established U.S. statehood before defining any shared identity or who would receive freedom.
insights.speaker: Elisabeth Leake
insights.so_what: Frames a recurring analytical lens — that U.S. foreign policy conflates 'independence' (statehood) with 'freedom' (rights) — useful for interpreting modern 'America First' rhetoric.

insights.claim: Historian David Armitage's framing, cited by Leake, holds that the Declaration was written as much for foreign European audiences — to persuade potential allies the American cause was a legitimate war for statehood, not mere rebellion — as for the colonists themselves.
insights.speaker: Elisabeth Leake
insights.so_what: Reframes the Declaration as an early exercise in public diplomacy aimed at securing foreign support — a precedent for reading U.S. foreign-policy documents as dual domestic/international messaging.
insights.quote: an appeal to the tribunal of the world
insights.locator: The declaration, as historian David Armitage

insights.claim: The declaration's 'all men are created equal' language excluded women, Black Americans, and Indigenous Americans in practice, and many states continued requiring property ownership for white male voters even after the Revolutionary War.
insights.speaker: Elisabeth Leake
insights.so_what: Establishes the baseline exclusionary reality behind founding-era rhetoric that recurs as a throughline in the essay's later foreign-policy analogies.

insights.claim: The Declaration's sole reference to Indigenous Americans branded them 'merciless Indian Savages' allied with the British — casting Native nations as enemies of the new state rather than as part of 'the people,' a framing that presaged 19th-century removal policy.
insights.speaker: Elisabeth Leake
insights.so_what: Direct textual evidence linking founding-era rhetoric to the later reservation system and Manifest Destiny.
insights.locator: One of the sparks for the revolution

insights.claim: Leake argues the 19th-century U.S. reservation system for Native Americans closely resembled British 'tribal' policy in India and West Africa, both isolating indigenous populations on inhospitable lands — making U.S. westward expansion structurally similar to the British imperial 'civilizing mission' it claimed to have repudiated.
insights.speaker: Elisabeth Leake
insights.so_what: A contrarian framing that undercuts American self-narrative of anti-imperialism, useful for geopolitical-history-informed views on U.S. exceptionalism claims.
insights.quote: bore a marked resemblance to British “tribal” policy in India and West Africa

insights.claim: Early U.S. statesmen including John Adams and Thomas Jefferson argued South American republics needed a 'stronger guiding hand' than the U.S. due to their large mixed-race and nonwhite populations, praising literacy and landownership voting restrictions that kept small white minorities in power.
insights.speaker: Elisabeth Leake
insights.so_what: Shows the racialized double standard embedded early in how U.S. elites viewed self-determination for other Western Hemisphere states.

themes.theme: Civil War amendments narrow the gap, on paper

insights.claim: The 13th, 14th, and 15th Amendments after the Civil War shifted U.S. law toward tying American identity to rights rather than race, expanding citizenship as a legal category — but Plessy v. Ferguson (1896) shows the promise of equal citizenship rights collapsed against state-level practice.
insights.speaker: Elisabeth Leake
insights.so_what: Documents the recurring pattern — legal expansion of rights followed by practical rollback — that the essay uses as the domestic mirror of U.S. foreign-policy hypocrisy on self-determination.
insights.locator: As demonstrated by Supreme Court cases

themes.theme: Cold War self-determination as an excuse

insights.claim: Since FDR's 1941 Atlantic Charter committed the U.S. to supporting self-determination abroad ('the right of all peoples to choose the form of government under which they will live'), U.S. officials largely limited this commitment to political independence, avoiding pressure on decolonizing states over internal freedom and equality because doing so would highlight U.S. domestic segregation and Native disenfranchisement.
insights.speaker: Elisabeth Leake
insights.so_what: Identifies the core Cold War-era policy mechanism — self-determination as statehood, not rights — that let Washington avoid pressuring allies on human rights, a pattern the essay claims persists.
insights.quote: the right of all peoples to choose the form of government under which they will live

insights.claim: President Truman's 1947 doctrine speech called flawed-democracy Greece a 'free nation' while calling undemocratic Turkey an 'independent and economically sound state' — using different terms for a similar lack of substantive freedom to justify supporting both regimes.
insights.speaker: Elisabeth Leake
insights.so_what: A concrete textual example showing how U.S. leaders semantically separated 'independence' from 'freedom' to justify supporting non-democratic allies — a template for reading current alliance rhetoric.
insights.locator: In articulating his eponymous doctrine in 1947

insights.claim: The Nixon administration treated Pakistan's 1971 genocide against Bengalis in East Pakistan as a purely internal sovereignty matter warranting no U.S. intervention, a stance Leake links to Pakistan's simultaneous role facilitating Nixon and Kissinger's opening to China.
insights.speaker: Elisabeth Leake
insights.so_what: A stark historical case of U.S. realpolitik overriding stated freedom principles when a client state offered strategic value — a precedent for weighing U.S. rhetoric against strategic interest in current allied-atrocity situations.

insights.claim: Leake argues recent U.S. interventions in Venezuela and Iran show 'America First' has drifted from its original protectionist, domestic-economy roots toward prioritizing U.S. strategic (state) interests abroad, even as domestic political polarization means Americans increasingly disagree on who or what counts as 'American.'
insights.speaker: Elisabeth Leake
insights.so_what: Suggests 'America First' is becoming a looser, more interventionist banner rather than a restraint on foreign engagement — relevant to expectations about future U.S. military/foreign-policy risk appetite.
